stem: On a motorway you should look ahead for the big picture about:

choices.text: 30 metres
100 metres
300 metres or more
Only as far as your headlights reach by day

explanation.short: Scan 300 m+ ahead on a motorway so you have time to react to trouble.
explanation.examTip: Combine far, mid and near scanning with regular mirror checks.

stem: A ball rolls onto the road from between parked cars. You should:

choices.text: Drive on at the same speed — there is nothing there yet
Sound the horn and continue
Lift off and prepare to brake for a child
Swerve sharply into the other lane

explanation.short: A ball predicts a child — slow down and be ready to stop.
explanation.examTip: Anticipating saves a hard brake or a collision later.

stem: You see brake lights three cars ahead on the motorway. You should:

choices.text: Ignore them — only the car directly ahead matters
Ease off the throttle and prepare to brake
Change lane immediately
Switch on hazard lights

explanation.short: Brake lights ahead are an early warning — react before the car directly in front brakes.
explanation.examTip: Use hazard lights only if a sudden traffic jam forms behind you.

stem: Approaching a junction in town with a cycle path on the right, you should:

choices.text: Speed up to clear the junction quickly
Slow down and check carefully for cyclists, including from the right
Sound the horn so cyclists know you are coming
Move to the middle of the road

explanation.short: Cyclists may appear from the right or swerve — slow down and look.
explanation.examTip: Cyclists from the right on a cycle path normally have priority.

stem: You are driving past a primary school at the end of the school day. You should:

choices.text: Drive at the normal speed limit
Slow well below the limit and expect unpredictable behaviour
Use the horn continuously
Drive close to the kerb to feel safer

explanation.short: Children are unpredictable and short; slow well below the limit near schools.
explanation.examTip: Watch for kids stepping between parked cars.

stem: Before changing lane on the motorway, the most important extra check for motorcyclists is:

choices.text: A glance in the rear-view mirror only
An over-the-shoulder check of the blind spot
Switching on the radio
Sounding the horn

explanation.short: Motorcyclists hide easily in the blind spot — always shoulder-check before changing lane.
explanation.examTip: Combine mirror + indicator + shoulder check, in that order.

stem: It starts to rain after several dry weeks. Compared with a normal wet road, the first 10–15 minutes are:

choices.text: Safer, because there is less standing water
About the same in grip
More slippery, because oil and dust are lifted to the surface
Drier in the wheel tracks

explanation.short: First rain after a dry spell creates an extra slippery film of oil and dust.
explanation.examTip: Increase following distance and brake earlier.

stem: Near freezing point, which part of the road is most likely to be icy even when the rest looks dry?

choices.text: A bridge or shaded patch
A south-facing slope in full sun
The middle of a wide motorway
A petrol station forecourt

explanation.short: Bridges and shadows freeze first because cold air gets to them from below or never warms them.
explanation.examTip: Watch the temperature display close to 0 °C.

stem: You are driving towards a low winter sun and the windscreen is dirty. The main risk is that:

choices.text: Other drivers will overtake you
The dirt scatters the light and hides pedestrians and cyclists in the glare
The car will overheat
The fuel will evaporate

explanation.short: A dirty screen scatters low sun glare and can completely hide people on the road.
explanation.examTip: Clean the screen, use the sun visor, and slow down.

stem: You see a wobbly cyclist 50 m ahead on a narrow road. The best first response is to:

choices.text: Maintain speed and pass quickly
Lift off in time so you have speed in hand if they swerve
Sound the horn until they stabilise
Flash the headlights

explanation.short: Having a few km/h in hand solves most cyclist surprises without drama.
explanation.examTip: Arriving 2 seconds later costs nothing; a collision costs everything.

stem: At 80 km/h on a wet road a hazard appears in your lane. As a rule, you should:

choices.text: Brake straight if you have time, only steer if braking will not be enough
Always swerve into the opposite lane
Always swerve onto the verge
Pull the handbrake

explanation.short: On a wet road, brake straight; swerving may lose grip and create a worse situation.
explanation.examTip: Only steer to avoid when there is a clear, safe space.

stem: While focusing fully on a cyclist ahead, many drivers cause an accident by:

choices.text: Driving too slowly afterwards
Missing a second hazard, such as a car braking in front
Using the indicator
Checking the mirrors

explanation.short: Tunnel-vision on one hazard makes you miss the next one — keep scanning.
explanation.examTip: Mirror checks during a reaction help you avoid rear-end collisions.

stem: You drive on a motorway and notice brake lights flashing two or three cars ahead. What is the best response?

choices.text: Maintain speed; only react when the car directly in front brakes
Ease off the accelerator and increase your following distance
Change lanes immediately without checking mirrors
Flash your headlights at the cars ahead

explanation.short: Reading several cars ahead lets you slow gently before you would otherwise have to brake hard.
explanation.examTip: This habit is the single biggest cause of avoiding motorway rear-end pile-ups.

stem: You signal to turn right at an urban junction. A cyclist is alongside your car on the right. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Turn quickly to get ahead of the cyclist
Wait until the cyclist has cleared the junction, then turn
Sound the horn and continue turning
Squeeze the cyclist toward the kerb

explanation.short: Right-turn 'dooring' or hooking is a classic killer of cyclists; wait until they are clear or visible well ahead of you.
explanation.examTip: Always do a final blind-spot check just before initiating the turn.

stem: On a wet motorway you feel the steering go light and the car begins to aquaplane. What should you do?

choices.text: Brake hard and steer sharply
Ease off the accelerator, hold the wheel straight and wait for grip to return
Accelerate to break through the water film
Pull the handbrake

explanation.short: Smoothly reduce speed and keep the wheel straight; the tyres will regain contact as speed drops.
explanation.examTip: Good tread depth and lower speed in standing water are the best prevention.

stem: You approach a junction where a parked van blocks your view of crossing traffic. The best policy is to:

choices.text: Maintain speed; the other driver must give way
Creep forward slowly until your view is clear, then proceed
Sound the horn and drive through
Close your eyes briefly and hope for the best

explanation.short: When your view is blocked, edge forward only as far as needed to see, then decide.
explanation.examTip: Pedestrians and cyclists may step out from behind the same obstacle — scan low and wide.

stem: Driving in town at 50 km/h, you notice brake lights flash on two cars ahead. The car directly in front of you has not braked yet. What is the safest immediate action?

choices.text: Wait until the car in front brakes, then react
Ease off the accelerator and cover the brake now
Accelerate to overtake before the queue forms
Sound your horn to warn other drivers

explanation.short: Brake lights further up are an early warning; preparing now avoids a late, hard brake later.
explanation.examTip: 'I expect — I prepare' beats 'I see — I brake' — anticipation is the key skill the exam tests.

stem: You are stopped at a red light, intending to turn right. A lorry pulls alongside you on your left, also indicating right. The light turns green. What should you do?

choices.text: Turn at the same time as the lorry — you both indicated
Accelerate to clear the junction before the lorry
Hold back and let the lorry complete the turn first
Sound your horn so the lorry waits

explanation.short: Lorries have a large right blind spot; any cyclist between you and the lorry is invisible to the lorry driver.
explanation.examTip: Never sit alongside a long vehicle at a junction where it could turn across you.

stem: On a motorway at 100 km/h in heavy rain, the steering suddenly feels light and the car begins to aquaplane. What is the correct response?

choices.text: Brake hard to stop quickly
Steer sharply to find dry tarmac
Lift off the accelerator, keep the wheel straight, and wait for grip
Pull the handbrake to slow the car

explanation.short: During aquaplaning, lift off, keep the wheel straight, and let the car slow until the tyres grip again.
explanation.examTip: Braking or steering during aquaplaning loses control — 'lift, straight, wait, slow'.

stem: On a dry rural road at 80 km/h, a small animal runs onto the road about 30 m ahead. There is oncoming traffic in the other lane and a ditch on your right. What is the safest decision?

choices.text: Swerve into the oncoming lane to avoid the animal
Swerve into the right-hand ditch
Brake hard in a straight line, accepting that you may hit the animal
Accelerate to pass the animal before it moves further

explanation.short: Brake straight in your own lane; swerving risks a head-on collision or losing the car off the road.
explanation.examTip: Brake first, steer only if braking alone is not enough and there is a clear, safe space to steer into.

stem: You are driving in the right lane of a motorway at 100 km/h. A heavily loaded lorry is accelerating slowly up an entry slip road on your right. What is the best prediction and action?

choices.text: Maintain your speed and lane — the lorry must wait
Check your mirror and blind spot, then move to the left lane if safe to ease the lorry in
Brake hard so the lorry can merge in front of you
Accelerate to pass before the lorry reaches the merging point

explanation.short: Cooperative merging keeps the flow safe; moving over (when safe) is preferred to sudden braking in the right lane.
explanation.examTip: Heavy vehicles need a lot of space to accelerate — predict their slow climb early.

stem: On a motorway during heavy rain you feel the steering go suddenly light and the car feels like it is floating. What should you do?

choices.text: Brake hard and steer sharply to regain grip
Release the accelerator, hold the steering straight and let speed drop
Accelerate to push the water out from under the tyres
Pull the handbrake to slow down

explanation.short: Aquaplaning is solved by smooth deceleration: release the throttle, no braking or steering input, wait for grip to return.
explanation.examTip: Aquaplaning risk rises sharply above 80 km/h on standing water — slow down before it happens.

stem: You are about to turn right at a junction inside a built-up area. You have just overtaken a cyclist who is now slightly behind you on your right. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Turn right immediately — you are now ahead
Check your right blind spot, slow down, and let the cyclist pass or clear before turning
Signal late so the cyclist does not slow you down
Sound the horn and turn anyway

explanation.short: The 'right hook' — turning right across a cyclist you just passed — is one of the deadliest urban scenarios; never cut across.
explanation.examTip: If in doubt, slow behind the cyclist, do not overtake, and turn after them.

stem: On a 80 km/h provincial road a car is tailgating you closely with full beams flashing. What is the correct decision?

choices.text: Brake hard to teach the driver a lesson
Hold your speed, increase the gap to the car ahead, and let the tailgater pass at the first safe opportunity
Speed up well above the limit to escape
Drive on the shoulder so the tailgater can pass

explanation.short: Never react aggressively to pressure; protect your own braking distance and remove the conflict by letting them past safely.
explanation.examTip: A bigger gap in front compensates for the smaller gap behind.

stem: On a narrow rural road you approach a blind right-hand bend. There are no warning signs, but the verge is muddy and there are fresh tractor tracks crossing the road. What should you predict and do?

choices.text: Maintain speed — there are no warning signs
Reduce speed and stay close to the centre line for visibility
Reduce speed, move slightly toward your own edge, and expect mud, a tractor or slow traffic round the bend
Switch to high beam to see past the bend

explanation.short: Tractor tracks and mud on a blind bend are strong cues for slow farm traffic and reduced grip — slow down and stay in your own half.
explanation.examTip: Reading the road surface for cues is a key advanced-hazard skill.

stem: On a motorway you are about to overtake a long articulated lorry on the left. The lorry's front indicator suddenly flashes left even though there is no exit. What is the most likely reason and what should you do?

choices.text: It is a mistake — accelerate past quickly
The lorry driver has seen something ahead and intends to change lane; back off and let them move first
The lorry's indicator is broken; ignore it
Move closer to the lorry's side to be visible

explanation.short: Lorry drivers have a higher viewpoint and often spot stationary or slow traffic before you do. An unexpected left indicator is a warning of a planned lane change.
explanation.examTip: Reading other drivers' intent — not just the road — is core to advanced hazard prediction.

stem: You wait to turn left out of a side road onto a busy main road. A long stream of oncoming cars passes; a small gap appears and you start to roll. A motorcycle is now visible between two cars in the gap. What does this teach about junctions?

choices.text: Motorcycles are always too slow to matter
Look twice and accept that a narrow vehicle can hide behind another until the last moment
Trust the first scan — a second look wastes time
Only check for cars; motorcycles will brake for you

explanation.short: A motorcycle can be completely hidden behind another vehicle. A second, dedicated look just before pulling out is the only reliable defence.
explanation.examTip: 'Look twice — save a life' — the Dutch SMV (Motorcyclist Visibility) message — applies at every side-road exit.

stem: On a clear morning in February you have driven on dry tarmac for 10 km. You approach a long bridge spanning a river. The dashboard shows 1 °C. Which surface is most likely to surprise you?

choices.text: The dry roadway on either side of the bridge
The bridge deck itself, because it cools faster than ground-supported road
The kerb stones beside the bridge
The painted markings only

explanation.short: Bridge decks have cold air above and below them, so they freeze before nearby road. At temperatures near zero, expect ice on bridges first.
explanation.examTip: Slow before the bridge, not on it — braking on a possibly icy span is what causes most crashes.

stem: On an 80 km/h rural road at dusk, a deer suddenly steps out 25 m ahead. You have a solid centre line, an oncoming car close on the other side and a deep ditch on your right. What is the safest decision?

choices.text: Swerve into the oncoming lane to miss the deer
Swerve right into the ditch
Brake firmly in a straight line and accept the collision if unavoidable
Accelerate to clear the deer's path

explanation.short: When swerving creates a worse outcome (head-on, ditch), brake hard in a straight line. A controlled collision with an animal is usually less severe than losing control.
explanation.examTip: ANWB and CBR teach: brake straight, do not swerve into oncoming traffic — your seatbelts and airbags are designed for forward impacts.

stem: On a 130 km/h motorway you crest a small rise and see brake lights flashing in the distance across all three lanes. You are not yet close. What does this combination of cues tell you?

choices.text: Drivers are being polite and slowing for no reason
There is a tailback or incident ahead; ease off, increase distance, and check your mirrors
The speed limit must have changed; no other action is required
It is safe to switch to the left lane and overtake

explanation.short: Brake lights across all lanes from a distance reliably indicate a tailback. Reading the wider traffic picture lets you slow gradually instead of braking hard at the back of the queue.
explanation.examTip: Look at the third or fourth car ahead, not the one in front of you — it is the early warning.

stem: On an open rural road in strong crosswind from the right, you approach a group of cyclists riding two-abreast on the cycle path. As you pass a gap in the hedge on the right, what extra risk should you expect?

choices.text: Cyclists may be blown sideways into the carriageway as the wind hits them
The cyclists will brake hard
The cycle path will become wider
No extra risk — the cycle path keeps them safe

explanation.short: Gusts at gaps in hedges or buildings can shove cyclists into the road. Widen your overtaking gap and slow down at these spots.
explanation.examTip: Predict where the wind will be strongest — open gates, field entrances, bridges — and adjust before you reach them.
